one_line_takeaway: An LLM-driven 'homotopy-inspired' paraphrase heuristic (not a formal optimization) reliably jailbreaks LLaMA, DeepSeek, and KIMI into producing verifiably malware-like code (75.8% precision, Claude-judged, over 9,725 attempts), and the authors release the resulting 7,374-sample dataset for defensive/detection research.
key_finding: Using Claude as judge (Table IV), on a total of 9,725 verified prompts the pipeline produced 7,374 confirmed malware specimens against 2,351 non-malicious outputs, for an aggregate precision/success rate of 0.758 (75.82%) and an error rate of 24.18%. Per-model breakdown (Claude-verified): LLaMA 320 malware / 180 non-malware = 64% precision (36% error rate, the weakest); DeepSeek 411/89 = 82.2% precision (17.8% error rate, the strongest); KIMI 6,643/2,082 = 76.13% precision (23.87% error rate). Using KIMI as judge instead (Table V), totals were 7,470 malware / 2,255 non-malware = 76.81% precision (23.19% error rate), with LLaMA 62.2%, DeepSeek 86.0%, KIMI 77.43%. The paper reports the relative disagreement between the two judges (Claude vs. KIMI) as 1.3%, characterized as minimal divergence. The conclusion states an overall jailbreak success rate of 76% across evaluated models.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a five-stage jailbreak pipeline that uses an LLM (KIMI) to perform 'homotopy-inspired' linguistic paraphrase of malware-related prompts, turning benign/sandboxed-sounding requests (e.g. containing qualifiers like 'simulation', 'mock', 'sandbox') into semantically equivalent but more explicit/malicious variants that are then submitted to LLaMA (CodeLlama-7b-hf), DeepSeek (deepseek-r1:7b), and KIMI (kimi-k2-0711-preview) for code generation, with outputs verified as malware or not by Claude (claude-sonnet-4-20250514) and cross-checked by KIMI as a second judge. The authors formalize the transformation as a discrete sequence of intermediate prompts constrained by a semantic-distance bound and an output-distribution divergence bound (KL/Jensen-Shannon), though they explicitly state the actual implementation is a heuristic LLM-driven paraphrase rather than a formal optimization (no distance metric or optimization function is actually computed, unlike the cited Functional Homotopy method of Wang et al., which the paper distinguishes itself from). The concrete deliverables are: (1) the obfuscation/elicitation framework itself, and (2) a released dataset of 7,374 LLM-generated, LLM-verified 'malware' specimens in C++/Python at https://github.com/Eduardolasso/Cybersecurity, intended for downstream cybersecurity research (e.g., malware classifier training).
threat_model: Attacker goal: elicit malware source code (e.g., trojans, ransomware, rootkits, keyloggers, RATs, etc., drawn from a curated glossary of 94 malware-related terms) from safety-aligned LLMs by bypassing their content-safety filters. Attack surface: text prompts submitted via each model's normal API/chat interface (no fine-tuning, no white-box access, no system-level exploitation). Mechanism: prompts are first phrased as benign/educational/sandboxed requests, then an LLM (KIMI) is instructed to 'apply a Homotopy deformation' -- producing a metaphorical/obfuscated paraphrase that keeps the same underlying functional intent while altering surface wording (e.g., stripping qualifiers like 'simulation', 'mock', 'sandbox' and substituting 'Generate', 'Real', 'Unsafe', 'No sandbox', 'Real Hardware') -- to evade keyword/intent-based safety filtering. Defender: the safety alignment and content filters built into LLaMA, DeepSeek, and KIMI; a separate LLM (Claude) is used purely as a post-hoc judge to verify whether generated outputs are actual malware, not as a defense being attacked. The paper explicitly scopes itself to text-only, single-turn prompt obfuscation against commodity code-generation LLMs; it does not target model weights, training pipelines, or multimodal/system-level vectors.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) malware classification relies exclusively on LLM-judge labeling of source code as 'malware-like', with no behavioral validation via real-world or sandboxed execution, so classifications may not correspond to functionally verified malware. (2) Evaluation was restricted to this one heuristic jailbreak family and does not cover the full space of adversarial techniques. (3) Scope is limited to text-based, single-language code generation (C++/Python), excluding multimodal or system-level attack vectors. (4) Prompt volume was unequal across models (KIMI got far more prompts than LLaMA/DeepSeek, due to compute/API access constraints), so cross-model precision comparisons should be interpreted cautiously since differences may reflect unequal exposure rather than true capability differences. (5) The authors note reported success/precision rates should be read as a lower bound, not exhaustive. (6) An operational failure occurred: 275 specimens were destroyed when a generated macro virus executed spontaneously in Excel during the KIMI-only verification pass, indicating imperfect isolation/sandboxing of the experimental pipeline itself.

datasets_used: Self-constructed prompt corpus: 15,732 benign prompts generated collectively by LLaMA, DeepSeek, and KIMI from a 94-term malware glossary; filtered to 10,000 for the obfuscation stage.
Self-constructed and released output dataset: 7,374 verified malware specimens (C++, C++20, Python) with behavioral descriptions, published at https://github.com/Eduardolasso/Cybersecurity.
quantitative_results: Claude-verified totals (Table IV): 7,374 TP (malware) / 2,351 FP (non-malware) out of 9,725, overall precision 0.758 (75.82%), error rate 24.18%.
Claude-verified per model: LLaMA 320/180 -> 64% precision, 36% error rate; DeepSeek 411/89 -> 82.2% precision, 17.8% error rate; KIMI 6,643/2,082 -> 76.13% precision, 23.87% error rate.
KIMI-verified totals (Table V): 7,470 TP / 2,255 FP out of 9,725 total, overall precision 0.7681 (76.81%), error rate 23.19%.

KIMI-verified per model: LLaMA 311/189 -> 62.2% precision, 37.8% error rate; DeepSeek 403/97 -> 86.0% precision (reported as 0.860 in table, text also cites 82.2/17.8% for the Claude table -- these are the two separate judge conditions), 19.4% error rate; KIMI 6,756/1,969 -> 77.43% precision, 22.57% error rate.
Inter-judge disagreement (Claude vs. KIMI as verifier): 1.3% relative error, described as minimal divergence.
Data loss event: 275 of 10,000 prompts lost when a generated macro virus executed in Excel during the first (KIMI-only) verification pass, reducing the working set to 9,725.
baselines_compared: No external prior jailbreak method is empirically re-run as a baseline in this paper; comparison to prior work (e.g., Functional Homotopy / Wang et al. 2024, and DAN/prompt-injection style jailbreaks surveyed in cited literature) is discussed only narratively, not benchmarked head-to-head.

The paper instead treats KIMI-as-judge versus Claude-as-judge as its internal cross-check/ablation, comparing the two verification results (Table IV vs. Table V) to assess judge-verification bias/circularity rather than comparing against an independent jailbreak baseline.
